title: Come hither, child by Emily Bronte — Sad Poetry Lines
content:
Come hither, child--who gifted thee
With power to touch that string so well?
How darest thou rouse up thoughts in me,
Thoughts that I would--but cannot quell?

Nay, chide not, lady; long ago
I heard those notes in Ula's hall,
And had I known they'd waken woe
meaning: "Come hither, child" by Emily Bronte is a sad and love and deep and nature poem consisting of 39 lines. The poet explores themes of sad and love and deep and nature through vivid imagery and emotional depth. Beginning with "Come hither, child--who gifted thee With power to touch that string so well?...", this piece captures the essence of human experience and invites reflection on the deeper currents of life and feeling. This work is part of Emily Bronte's celebrated body of poetry that continues to resonate with readers worldwide.
